issue_title: SendDecline side effect with multiple interface on the same network
issue_body:
**systemd version the issue has been seen with**
> systemd 246 (246)
> +PAM -AUDIT -SELINUX +IMA -APPARMOR +SMACK +SYSVINIT +UTMP +LIBCRYPTSETUP +GCRYPT +GNUTLS +ACL -XZ +LZ4 +ZSTD +SECCOMP +BLKID +ELFUTILS +KMOD +IDN2 -IDN +PCRE2 default-hierarchy=hybrid

**Used distribution**
 > Gentroo

**Linux kernel version used** (`uname -a`)
 > Linux lady-voodoo.local 5.4.67-longterm #1 SMP PREEMPT 2020-10-01T23:33:09+01:00 x86_64 Intel(R) Core(TM) i7-4500U CPU @ 1.80GHz GenuineIntel GNU/Linux

**CPU architecture issue was seen on**
 > x86_64

When a system has 2 network interfaces (here Ethernet and WiFi) connected into the same L2 network, both configured with `[DHCPv4] SendDecline=true`, systemd-networkd identifies both IP as DAD. This issue can also be observed when only one of the 2 interface has `SendDecline` enabled, I will use this here to simplify the bug report.

```
Oct 02 16:58:40 lady-voodoo.local systemd-networkd[14730]: eth0: DHCPv4 address 10.134.1.141/25 via 10.134.1.129
Oct 02 16:58:40 lady-voodoo.local systemd-networkd[14730]: eth0: DAD conflict. Dropping DHCP4 address 10.134.1.141
Oct 02 16:58:40 lady-voodoo.local systemd-networkd[14730]: eth0: DHCP lease lost
```

Looking a bit more into details about this, I can see the following is observed from a network capture:

* Interface L2 info:

```
2: eth0: <BROADCAST,MULTICAST,UP,LOWER_UP> mtu 1500 qdisc fq state UP mode DEFAULT group default qlen 1000
    link/ether 24:f5:aa:c7:86:a6 brd ff:ff:ff:ff:ff:ff
3: wlan0: <BROADCAST,MULTICAST,UP,LOWER_UP> mtu 1500 qdisc noqueue state UP mode DORMANT group default qlen 1000
    link/ether 28:b2:bd:c0:42:e8 brd ff:ff:ff:ff:ff:ff
```

* Traffic on interface #1 (eth0)
```
$ sudo tcpdump -i eth0 -nn -vv -s 0 -e arp and not host 10.134.1.129 --direction=out
..
16:57:48.783829 24:f5:aa:c7:86:a6 > ff:ff:ff:ff:ff:ff, ethertype ARP (0x0806), length 42: Ethernet (len 6), IPv4 (len 4), Request who-has 10.134.1.141 tell 0.0.0.0, length 28
16:57:48.993494 24:f5:aa:c7:86:a6 > ff:ff:ff:ff:ff:ff, ethertype ARP (0x0806), length 42: Ethernet (len 6), IPv4 (len 4), Request who-has 10.134.1.141 tell 0.0.0.0, length 28
..
```
```
$ sudo tcpdump -i eth0 -nn -vv -s 0 -e arp and not host 10.134.1.129 --direction=in
<empty>
```

* Traffic on interface #2 (wlan0)

```
$ sudo tcpdump -i wlan0 -nn -vv -s 0 -e arp and not host 10.134.1.129 --direction=out
<empty>
```
```
$ sudo tcpdump -i wlan0 -nn -vv -s 0 -e arp and not host 10.134.1.129 --direction=in
..
16:57:48.861772 24:f5:aa:c7:86:a6 > ff:ff:ff:ff:ff:ff, ethertype ARP (0x0806), length 60: Ethernet (len 6), IPv4 (len 4), Request who-has 10.134.1.141 tell 0.0.0.0, length 46
16:57:49.065638 24:f5:aa:c7:86:a6 > ff:ff:ff:ff:ff:ff, ethertype ARP (0x0806), length 60: Ethernet (len 6), IPv4 (len 4), Request who-has 10.134.1.141 tell 0.0.0.0, length 46
..
```

What is happening here is the following:
* An ARP request is broadcasted on `eth0` to identify a duplicate (this is expected)
* The ARP request is reaching all devices on the L2 network where `eth0` is connected (this is expected)
* Which means, the second interface (wlan0) is also receiving the ARP request with eth0 MAC address as the source (this is expected)
* systemd-networkd identifies this ARP packet as a DAD, this is not expected since the source MAC of the incoming packet is the MAC address of the initial interface sending the packet out
